# Aachen Public Transport Coverage — Model, Methodology & Results

*Business Analytics Project, SoSe 2026. Everything below is generated by the
validated pipeline (`aachen_model.py`) on the real data and is ready to write up.*

---

## 1. What we do (one paragraph for the abstract)

We build on the Katsioupis (2026) p-median baseline for siting new bus stops in
Aachen and extend it into a **cost-aware, equity-conscious decision-support
analysis**. Instead of the abstract "choose *p* stops", we introduce an explicit
**heterogeneous construction cost** per candidate (grounded in German
barrier-free stop-construction figures) and a real **euro budget**, and we add a
distributional lens: an explicit target for the currently **underserved central
population** (residents who today walk more than 300 m to the nearest stop).
Across a scenario sweep we compare a cost-blind and a cost-aware investment plan,
quantify the **price of equity**, and derive a **cost-effectiveness–based
recommendation** for Aachen — the practical, KPI-driven conclusion the baseline
never draws.

---

## 2. The model (methods chapter)

**Sets.** demand nodes `i ∈ I` (16,984 residential cells, 50×50 m); existing
stops `j ∈ Jᵉ` (1,037); candidate stops `j ∈ Jᶜ` (141).

**Data / parameters.** population `popᵢ`; central flag; walking distance
`dᵢⱼ = f · euclidean`, circuity `f = 1.3`; sparsified by the **dominance rule**
(a candidate is kept for node *i* only if it strictly beats *i*'s nearest
existing stop → 5,197 relevant candidate pairs, exactly the baseline's count);
per-candidate cost `costⱼ` (§3); underserved set `U = {i : central, base walk > 300 m}`.

**Decision variables.** `xⱼ ∈ {0,1}` open candidate *j*; `yᵢⱼ ∈ {0,1}` node *i*
served by stop *j*.

**Formulation.**

```
min  Σ_i Σ_j  popᵢ · dᵢⱼ · yᵢⱼ                     (population-weighted walking distance)
s.t. Σ_j yᵢⱼ = 1                     ∀i             (each node served once)
     yᵢⱼ ≤ xⱼ                        ∀i, j∈Jᶜ       (coupling: use only opened candidates)
     yᵢⱼ ≤ Aᵢⱼ                                       (respect sparsified adjacency)
     Σ_{j∈Jᶜ} costⱼ · xⱼ ≤ B                         (★ EURO BUDGET — replaces "≤ p")
     Σ_{i∈U} Σ_{j: dᵢⱼ≤300} popᵢ · yᵢⱼ ≥ τ · pop(U)  (★ EQUITY floor, optional)
```

The two ★ lines are our additions; everything else is the baseline p-median.
Setting `costⱼ ≡ 1`, `B = p`, `τ = 0` recovers Katsioupis exactly.

**Solver note.** Once the `xⱼ` are fixed the assignment is naturally integral, so
`yᵢⱼ` may be declared **continuous** — this keeps the MIP light (only 141 binary
variables) and each scenario solves in seconds. Provided both in pure Python
(PuLP/CBC, portable) and GAMSPy (handout requirement); both give identical plans.

---

## 3. Cost model (data chapter — the one modelling choice to defend)

Grounded in reported German figures for barrier-free stop construction
(typically **€40k–50k**, range **€20k–100k** per stop/direction; drivers: stop
length Buskap ~20 m vs Busbucht up to 50 m, excavation, curb setting, real-time
displays). We set, per candidate (k€):

| component | value | rationale |
|---|---|---|
| base barrier-free rebuild | 45 | mandatory accessible standard (Buskap) |
| + solo surcharge | 15 | isolated stop pays full mobilisation (no shared bidirectional pair, < 40 m) |
| + demand-load tier | 0 / 20 / 40 | busier stops need longer platform + real-time display |

→ per-candidate cost **€45k–100k**, mean €74.3k. The mix of a *geometry* driver
(pairing, demand-independent) and a *demand* driver makes the cost-aware plan
genuinely differ from the cost-blind one. **Robustness** is shown by the cost
sensitivity analysis (§4.5); the conclusions hold when the surcharges are halved
or increased by 50 %.

---

## 4. Results (results chapter — all numbers real)

### 4.0 Baseline validation (proves our core = Katsioupis)
Status quo (no new stops): avg walking **192.38 m**, global coverage **61.44 %**
@200 m / **94.71 %** @400 m / **98.39 %** @600 m; central coverage 68.15 %
@200 m. These reproduce Katsioupis to the decimal — our engine is his engine.
*(Note of scope: all 141 candidates lie in the central area, so new stops move
the central KPIs; global KPIs move little. State this explicitly.)*

### 4.1 / 4.2 Cost–accessibility trade-off, cost-blind vs cost-aware
`results/01_pareto_costblind.csv`, `results/02_pareto_costaware.csv`

| budget | stops | avg walk | central cov@200 | central cov@300 |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| €0 | 0 | 192.38 m | 68.15 % | 91.36 % |
| €180k | 2 | 190.72 m | 70.74 % | 93.45 % |
| €360k | 4 | 189.28 m | 73.61 % | 94.53 % |
| €540k | 6 | 187.95 m | 75.96 % | 95.64 % |
| €900k | 11 | 185.74 m | 80.27 % | 96.94 % |
| €1,200k | 14 | 184.48 m | 82.96 % | 97.60 % |

**Key finding (not relabelling):** the cost-blind and cost-aware plans select
**different stops** at nearly every budget. E.g. at €135k, cost-blind builds
{1050}; cost-aware builds {1045, 1072} — two stops for the same money. At €45k,
cost-blind can afford nothing (mean price €74k) while cost-aware already builds
the cheap stop 1065. Accounting for heterogeneous costs changes *which* stops get
built and lets the city act earlier and reach more people per euro.

### 4.3 The price of equity
`results/03_equity_scenarios.csv` — minimum budget to cover τ % of the 6,032
underserved central residents within 300 m:

| cover τ of underserved | min budget | stops | system avg walk |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 30 % | €230k | 3 | 190.23 m |
| 50 % | €450k | 6 | 188.95 m |
| 70 % | €890k | 11 | 186.29 m |

**Finding:** the underserved can be substantially helped cheaply — 30 % coverage
for €230k — and doing so barely costs system-wide efficiency (avg walk stays
~190 m). Equity and efficiency are largely *complementary* here, not opposed. The
theoretical ceiling is 77.9 % (some underserved cells have no candidate within
300 m), which is itself a planning insight.

### 4.4 Cost-effectiveness & recommendation
`results/05_cost_effectiveness.csv`. Marginal cost per additional
central-coverage-point stays low up to ~€450k, then rises steeply. **Recommended
plan for Aachen: ≈ €450k, 5 stops, central coverage@300 = 95.2 %** — the point
where marginal euros stop buying meaningful accessibility.

### 4.5 Cost-parameter sensitivity
`results/06_cost_sensitivity.csv`. Scaling the cost surcharges by 0.5× / 1.0× /
1.5× changes the €360k plan by at most one stop and central coverage@300 stays
94.2–95.3 %. Conclusions are robust to the cost assumption.

### 4.6 Reference: target-driven scenarios (Katsioupis S1–S4)
`results/04_targetdriven_S1_S4.csv` — cheapest budget to hit each central
coverage target, for direct comparison with the baseline's scenarios.

### 4.7 Cost–benefit appraisal (the deep economic layer)
`results/07_per_stop_NKV.csv`, `08_plan_comparison_CBA.csv`, `09_CBA_sensitivity.csv`

Walking-time savings are **monetised** with the German *Standardisierte
Bewertung* (Formblatt 20: **6.60 €/person-hour**), discounted over a 30-year life
(1.7 %, BVWP 2030), and set against construction cost as a **Nutzen-Kosten-
Verhältnis (NKV / benefit-cost ratio)**. An **NKV ≥ 1.0 is the statutory GVFG
funding threshold** — a real, citable decision rule. We also add a
**net-benefit-maximising** model (`max Σ benefitᵢⱼ·yᵢⱼ − Σ costⱼ·xⱼ`), which opens
a stop only if its monetised benefit beats its cost — a genuine cost-benefit
facility-location problem, not a p-median with a budget cap.

**Findings.**
- **136 of 141 candidate stops are individually fundable (NKV ≥ 1)**; 5 are not
  and should not be built — a concrete recommendation Katsioupis cannot give.
  Per-stop NKV ranges 0.63–15.9 (median 5.9).
- The four planning philosophies, appraised on one footing:

| plan | stops | cost | benefit | NKV | central cov@300 | underserved cov@300 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| cost-aware (€360k) | 4 | €350k | €3.9M | **11.2** | 94.5 % | 36.7 % |
| equity-first (50 %) | 6 | €450k | €4.3M | 9.6 | 95.7 % | 50.3 % |
| net-benefit-optimal | 43 | €3.44M | €15.0M | 4.4 | 98.0 % | 76.6 % |

  → a few stops give the highest *return* (NKV 11), but the economically optimal
  programme is much larger (43 stops) and still returns €4.4 per €1, lifting the
  underserved to the 77.9 % ceiling.
- **Robustness:** even under pessimistic assumptions (75 trips/yr, 3 % discount)
  the programme NKV stays 2.6 and 24 stops remain fundable (`09_CBA_sensitivity`).

*Key assumption to firm up:* home-end transit-access walks per resident per year
(default 150 ≈ 0.4/day). The sensitivity spans 75–300; conclusions hold. VTTS and
discount rate are the official appraisal values.

### 4.8 POI accessibility: schools & hospitals (Meeting-Summary requirement)
`results/10_poi_stop_access.csv`, `10b_poi_detail.csv`, `12_final_plan_comparison.csv`

18 POIs (5 hospitals, 13 schools; `pois_aachen.csv`, manually compiled from
public map data, replaceable 1:1 by an OSM Overpass export). Two perspectives:

- **Facility side:** 83.3 % of POIs already have a stop within 300 m walking
  (mean 212 m) — the *institutions* are well connected. The gap is elsewhere:
- **Catchment side:** 42,861 residents live within 400 m of a school/hospital;
  **3,405 of them are underserved** (stop walk > 300 m). Of these, only 43.9 %
  are fixable at all with the current candidate set — itself a planning finding
  (the candidate set was not designed with POI access in mind).
- **POI-priority plan:** covering 35 % of the underserved catchment costs just
  **€195k (3 stops, NKV 11.2)** — the cheapest of all plans, with near-top
  cost-effectiveness.

### 4.9 The final integrated comparison (the paper's centrepiece table)
`results/12_final_plan_comparison.csv`, figure `final_who_benefits.png`

| plan | stops | cost | NKV | central cov@300 | underserved cov@300 | POI-underserved cov@300 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| status quo | 0 | 0 | – | 91.4 % | 0 % | 0 % |
| cost-aware (€360k) | 4 | €350k | 11.2 | 94.5 % | 36.7 % | 33.4 % |
| equity-first (50 %) | 6 | €450k | 9.6 | 95.7 % | 50.3 % | 35.2 % |
| POI-priority (35 %) | 3 | €195k | 11.2 | 93.3 % | 22.2 % | 35.1 % |
| net-benefit-optimal | 43 | €3.44M | 4.4 | 98.0 % | 76.6 % | 43.9 % |

**The story in one sentence:** the same city, the same candidate stops, but four
defensible value systems — pure efficiency, distributional fairness, essential-
services access, and economic net benefit — produce visibly different plans; our
framework makes that trade-off explicit and quantifies each option's price,
return (NKV) and beneficiaries, which is exactly the decision support the
baseline could not provide.

### Figures (`figures/`)
`pareto_cost_coverage.png` (cost-blind vs cost-aware), `avgwalk_budget.png`
(diminishing returns), `equity_tradeoff.png` (price of equity).

---

## 5. Paper structure (maps to Handout Tasks I–V)

1. **Introduction** — accessibility gap in Aachen; from "how much" to "at what
   cost and for whom". (Task I framing)
2. **Regulatory context** — PBefG §8(3): barrier-free stops mandatory; AVV
   walking-distance thresholds (200/300/400 m). (Task II)
3. **Data** — demand grid, existing/candidate stops, dominance sparsification,
   the cost model (§3). (Task III)
4. **Model** — formulation (§2), baseline vs our two extensions. (Task I)
5. **KPIs** — the panel: coverage (global/central/underserved), avg walking
   distance, #stops, cost, affected pop, cost-effectiveness, equity gap. (Task IV)
6. **Numerical experiments** — §4.0–4.6, current vs optimised. (Task V)
7. **Discussion** — cost-aware ≠ cost-blind; price of equity; recommendation.
8. **Limitations & conclusion** — cost schema is modelled not surveyed; static
   residential demand; candidates confined to central area; stop placement only
   (no routing/frequency).

---

## 6. What system to run it in, and what data you still need

**System.**
- *Deliverable / handout requirement:* **GAMSPy** — `aachen_model_gamspy.py`,
  run with your `.gamspy_venv`. This is the version you cite as "implemented in
  GAMSPy".
- *Analysis / reproducibility:* **pure Python** — `aachen_model.py`
  (`pip install pandas numpy scipy pulp matplotlib`). This one the supervisor can
  run with no GAMS licence and it regenerates every table and figure. Ship both.
- No GAMS MIRO app — per the supervisor, that direction is dropped.

**Data — you already have everything needed to run** (existing.csv,
candidates.csv, demand.csv, distances_e_new.csv, distances_c_new.csv). Two
optional strengtheners, only if you want to go further:
1. **Cost schema confirmation** — the €45–100k scheme is defensible from public
   figures, but a one-line sanity check with the supervisor / AVV / ASEAG would
   make it bullet-proof. The sensitivity analysis already covers the uncertainty.
2. **Richer equity subgroup (optional)** — we currently define "underserved" from
   baseline walking distance (needs no extra data). If you want the stronger
   "access to schools / clinics / vulnerable districts" story from the meeting,
   add OSM POIs or the `aachen_districts.gpkg` and flag those nodes; the model
   takes any node subgroup unchanged.

**One open question for the supervisor** (the confirmation email): is extending
the baseline with this cost + equity + cost-effectiveness analysis accepted as
the contribution, or does she expect a structurally new optimisation problem?
Everything above assumes the former, which matches the meeting summary.
